primary_diagnosis.name: Acute decompensated congestive heart failure with pulmonary oedema, precipitated by missed diuretic doses

pathophysiology_summary: This is acute decompensated congestive heart failure with pulmonary oedema in an older aged-care resident, precipitated by an abrupt loss of diuresis (three missed days of furosemide from a prescription-refill error). Without the diuretic, sodium and water are retained, intravascular and interstitial volume rise, and left ventricular filling pressures increase and transmit back into the pulmonary circulation, producing interstitial and alveolar oedema experienced as severe orthopnoea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea and objectively as bilateral basilar crackles extending to the mid-zones and resting hypoxaemia (SpO2 91%). Rising right-heart filling pressures elevate the JVP (8 cm) and drive massive dependent bilateral pitting oedema (to the sacrum) with rapid weight gain (5 kg). A third heart sound (S3) gallop reflects rapid diastolic filling of a volume-overloaded ventricle, a displaced apex indicates cardiomegaly, and hepatomegaly with a positive hepatojugular reflux confirms systemic venous congestion. The tachypnoea with accessory-muscle use signals significant pulmonary oedema and impending respiratory failure. The treatment is prompt, potent diuresis (typically intravenous furosemide) with oxygen titrated to target saturations and monitoring for the need for non-invasive ventilation, alongside restarting the regular diuretic and correcting the refill error. IV diuresis, oxygen titration, monitoring and possible NIV cannot be delivered by usual facility oral measures over telehealth, so urgent in-person medical assessment/transfer is required.

differential.diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure with pulmonary oedema
differential.evidence_basis: Suffocating orthopnoea, PND and massive leg swelling in a known heart-failure patient who missed diuretics is a classic acute decompensation from the first turn.
Breathlessness could be respiratory, but the absence of wheeze with an S3, raised JVP and massive fluid weight gain points to a cardiogenic cause.
Considered for breathlessness, but no fever/purulent sputum and the bilateral dependent crackles with oedema favour cardiogenic pulmonary oedema.
Acute breathlessness warrants considering PE, but the gradual congestive build-up with a clear diuretic-cessation precipitant makes decompensated heart failure far more likely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the outset the AI Doctor should frame this as acute decompensated heart failure with a clear precipitant (missed diuretic) and screen the congestion tetrad.

differential.diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure with pulmonary oedema (precipitated by missed diuretic)
differential.evidence_basis: Severe orthopnoea (DI-001) + PND (DI-002) + massive oedema/rapid weight gain (DI-003) + missed diuretic doses (DI-004), with resting hypoxaemia, is a textbook decompensation with an identified reversible precipitant.
No wheeze; the cardiogenic congestion pattern dominates.
No fever or purulent sputum; findings are dependent congestion, not consolidation.
Kept in mind but far less likely given the congestive picture and clear precipitant.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the AI Doctor should be confident this is acute decompensated heart failure/pulmonary oedema needing urgent potent diuresis and oxygen, with the missed diuretic identified as the reversible cause.

differential.diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure with pulmonary oedema (missed-diuretic precipitant) - presumptive, pending BNP/bloods and echocardiography
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Confirmation with BNP and bloods; safe potent diuresis, oxygen titration and monitoring for NIV require in-person medical assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is acute pulmonary oedema needing urgent same-day medical assessment/transfer for IV diuresis and monitoring, plus restarting the diuretic and fixing the refill error - not simply giving the missed tablets and waiting for the GP round.

red_flags.description: Severe orthopnoea with a sensation of drowning when lying flat
red_flags.clinical_significance: Severe orthopnoea reflects marked pulmonary congestion and significant decompensation requiring urgent treatment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to florid pulmonary oedema and respiratory failure overnight.

red_flags.description: Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea - waking gasping for air
red_flags.clinical_significance: PND is a hallmark of decompensated heart failure indicating advanced congestion.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A further nocturnal episode escalates to acute pulmonary oedema without timely response.

red_flags.description: Massive bilateral pitting oedema to the thighs/sacrum with 5 kg rapid weight gain and resting hypoxaemia (SpO2 91%)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rapid fluid overload with resting hypoxaemia quantifies severe decompensation and impending respiratory failure.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing accumulation worsens pulmonary oedema toward respiratory arrest.

red_flags.description: Missed three days of furosemide (facility prescription-refill error) - reversible precipitant
red_flags.clinical_significance: Identifying the missed diuretic pinpoints the reversible cause; the diuretic must be restarted and the systemic refill error corrected.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to correct the precipitant allows recurrent decompensation.

red_flags.description: Elevated JVP (8 cm), S3 gallop, displaced apex, hepatomegaly with positive hepatojugular reflux, and bibasal crackles to the mid-zones on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: These clinician-elicited signs confirm biventricular congestion and cardiomegaly; none are obtainable by the patient over telehealth (though nursing staff assist with vitals).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - clinician examination is required; the correct action is urgent in-person medical assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-treating by simply giving the missed tablets and waiting
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Restarting the missed oral diuretic is necessary but insufficient for acute pulmonary oedema with resting hypoxaemia, which usually needs potent (IV) diuresis, oxygen titration and monitoring.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Mislabelling as a chest infection or COPD flare
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Breathlessness in an older resident can be attributed to infection or airways disease; the S3, raised JVP and massive fluid gain indicate cardiogenic pulmonary oedema.
The refill error that caused the missed doses must be corrected to prevent recurrence - a system issue as well as a clinical one.
Leg heaviness and nocturnal toileting create a high fall risk that must be managed alongside the cardiac emergency.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cardiac auscultation for the S3 gallop and apex position
Assessment of JVP height and hepatojugular reflux
Arterial blood gases and precise oxygenation trend
Intravenous diuretic administration and titration with monitoring
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person medical assessment (facility medical officer or emergency-department transfer) for potent/IV diuresis, oxygen titrated to target saturations and monitoring for non-invasive ventilation; restart the regular furosemide immediately and correct the prescription-refill error; ambulance transfer if breathing worsens, SpO2 falls below 90% on oxygen, or the patient becomes confused or lethargic.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Simply giving the missed tablets and waiting for the routine GP round risks progression to florid pulmonary oedema and respiratory failure in a hypoxaemic, resting-breathless patient.
